evals.name: audit-seeded-dp
evals.prompt: Audit how our system handles data against the Singapore government data protection requirements. I've included our Terraform (main.tf), a doc describing our data handling practices (DATA-HANDLING.md), and the case entry form template (templates/case_form.html). Give us an audit report (audit.md) with pass/fail per requirement and how to fix the failures.
evals.expected_output: An audit report keyed to DP control IDs (DP-1..DP-8) that catches the seeded violations: CONFIDENTIAL data replicated to us-east-1 via S3 CRR and overseas backup copies (DP-1), an unencrypted RDS instance and unencrypted EBS (DP-2), plaintext internal HTTP and a Postgres connection with TLS disabled excused as 'private VPC' (DP-3), a standalone AWS account outside the agency's GCC organisation (DP-4), an office NAS quick-formatted and sold without sanitisation standard or witnessed destruction (DP-5/DP-6), no DLP (DP-7), and no classification disclosure on the officer-facing input form (DP-8). It should correct the 'PDPA doesn't apply to us so obligations are lighter' claim (public agencies are excluded from PDPA but the PSGA and ICT&SS/IM8 regime still binds), attribute the MD5 password hashing to the AS/CK families rather than DP, and pair every failure with a concrete fix.
assertions: Report catches at least 7 of the 8 seeded DP violations listed in seeded_violations
Findings cite ICT&SS Data Protection control IDs (DP-1 through DP-8)

Flags the DP-1 residency breaches: S3 cross-region replication of CONFIDENTIAL case data to us-east-1 AND backup copies sent to an overseas region for DR - with a fix that pins data to Singapore (ap-southeast-1) and enforces it with a guardrail (e.g. an SCP/policy denying non-SG regions), not just convention

Flags the DP-2 at-rest encryption failures: the RDS instance with storage_encrypted = false (and the unencrypted EBS volume), with a fix that validates encryption per service rather than assuming CSP defaults

Flags the DP-3 in-transit failures: plaintext HTTP between internal services and the Postgres connection with TLS not enforced, and explicitly rejects the 'it's inside a private VPC so it doesn't need TLS' justification

Flags the DP-4 failure: the workload runs in a standalone AWS account outside the agency's central GCC / AWS Organizations structure, with a fix that brings it under the central tenancy with landing-zone guardrails

Flags the DP-5/DP-6 disposal failure: the office NAS was quick-formatted and sold - a quick format is not a recognised sanitisation standard (e.g. Gutmann / Schneier / DoD 5220.22-M), retirement-bound storage should be shredded or incinerated, and no agency staff witnessed the process under a documented SOP

Flags the missing DLP (DP-7) with a fix naming a real mechanism (e.g. Microsoft Purview, Google Workspace DLP, or Amazon Macie-based blocking) that monitors flows, detects sensitive transfers, and blocks unauthorised sharing

Flags the missing DP-8 disclosure: the internal officer-facing case form has no message at or near the input fields stating the highest permitted security/sensitivity classification, and the fix adds it in the UI and the user guide

Corrects the PDPA claim: public agencies are excluded from the PDPA, but that does NOT lighten obligations - the Public Sector (Governance) Act and the ICT&SS/IM8 controls still bind (and vendors carry obligations contractually); the report does not accept the exclusion as a reason to skip any DP control
Attributes the MD5 password hashing to the Application Security / Cryptography families (AS/CK, secure-coding-as) rather than to a DP control, and does not fabricate DP controls that do not exist
Report is structured as pass/fail (or fail/not-configured) per requirement with a concrete fix for every failure, as the prompt asked
seeded_violations: S3 cross-region replication rule copies the CONFIDENTIAL case-documents bucket to us-east-1, and AWS Backup copy jobs send backups to us-east-1 'for geographic resilience' - government data leaving Singapore (DP-1)
RDS Postgres instance has storage_encrypted = false and the worker EBS volume has encrypted = false (DP-2)
Internal service-to-service traffic is plain HTTP and the Postgres parameter group sets rds.force_ssl = 0, justified in the doc as fine because the VPC is private (DP-3)
The workload runs in a standalone AWS account opened by the team outside the agency's GCC / AWS Organizations structure, with no landing-zone guardrails (DP-4)

A decommissioned office NAS that held case exports was quick-formatted and sold second-hand - no recognised sanitisation standard, no shred/incinerate, no witnessed destruction or SOP (DP-5 and DP-6, counted as one seeded violation)
No data loss prevention anywhere - nothing monitors flows, detects sensitive transfers, or blocks unauthorised sharing (DP-7)
The internal officer-facing case entry form has no classification message at or near its inputs and the user guide says nothing about a ceiling (DP-8)
The doc claims that because the agency is government, the PDPA does not apply and data protection obligations are therefore lighter - the PSGA + ICT&SS/IM8 regime still binds (PDPA-exclusion trap)
Passwords hashed with MD5 - an Application Security / Cryptography (AS/CK) issue included as a cross-family distractor that must NOT be attributed to a DP control

evals.name: setup-dp-baseline
evals.prompt: We're building an internal case management system for public officers at a Singapore government agency, on AWS under our GCC tenancy, handling data up to CONFIDENTIAL / Sensitive Normal. There's also one on-prem scanning appliance with local storage that we'll eventually retire. Our SSP sets: data residency = Singapore for all government data on the system; central tenancy = all agency systems under the agency's AWS Organizations structure in GCC. Set up our data protection so it meets the government's requirements: the residency guardrails, encryption, tenancy placement, the storage disposal SOP, data loss prevention, and what the input screens must tell officers.
evals.expected_output: A data-protection baseline keyed to DP control IDs: Singapore residency enforced by guardrail (SCP/region pinning) with replication/backup/DR kept in-region (DP-1); at-rest encryption validated per service across volumes, snapshots, objects, DB, queues, caches (DP-2); TLS on external and internal hops including the database connection (DP-3); the workload inside the GCC AWS Organizations structure with landing-zone guardrails (DP-4); a sanitisation-and-witnessed-destruction SOP for the on-prem appliance using recognised standards with agency-staff witness records (DP-5/DP-6); DLP that monitors, detects, and blocks via a real mechanism (DP-7); and a classification disclosure at or near every input field and in the user guide matching the CONFIDENTIAL / Sensitive Normal ceiling (DP-8) - with algorithm/key choices scoped to the CK family rather than invented here.
assertions: Enforces Singapore residency with a technical guardrail - e.g. an SCP or policy denying non-SG regions / pinning to ap-southeast-1 - and keeps replication, backups, and DR in-region rather than relying on convention (DP-1)
Cites ICT&SS Data Protection control IDs (DP-1 through DP-8) to justify the setup
Configures at-rest encryption validated per service - covering block volumes and snapshots, object storage, the database, and queues/caches - rather than assuming CSP defaults (DP-2)

Requires TLS on every hop including internal service-to-service traffic and the database connection (e.g. rds.force_ssl / require_secure_transport), explicitly not exempting traffic inside the VPC (DP-3)
Places the workload inside the agency's GCC / AWS Organizations structure with landing-zone guardrails and centralised logging, and warns against standalone accounts outside it (DP-4)
Provides a sanitisation SOP for the on-prem scanning appliance using a recognised standard (e.g. Gutmann, Schneier, DoD 5220.22-M) with shredding/incineration for retirement-bound storage (DP-5)
Requires an agency staff member to witness the sanitisation/destruction under a documented SOP with per-device records - noting a vendor certificate alone is insufficient (DP-6)

Sets up data loss prevention that monitors data flows, detects sensitive transfers, and blocks unauthorised sharing, via a real mechanism (e.g. Microsoft Purview, Google Workspace DLP, Amazon Macie-based blocking) tuned to the classifications in play (DP-7)
Specifies the highest permitted classification (CONFIDENTIAL / Sensitive Normal) at or near every input field in the officer-facing UI and in the user guide (DP-8)

Scopes encryption algorithm and key management choices to the Cryptography/Key Management (CK) family (secure-coding-as) rather than inventing them here, and does not fabricate DP controls that do not exist

evals.name: assess-saas-data-boundary
evals.prompt: An agency team wants to use a SaaS case-collaboration product. The vendor hosts the primary tenant in Singapore but sends telemetry to Australia, stores support attachments in the US, keeps disaster-recovery copies in Japan, controls its own cloud accounts, and promises encryption without identifying which data stores or internal hops are covered. Officers can paste CONFIDENTIAL / Sensitive Normal case details into free-text fields, and exported files can be shared through public links. Assess the proposal against DP-1 through DP-8 and produce a decision record listing pass, fail, evidence needed, compensating design, and agency/SSP decisions. Distinguish a vendor-managed SaaS boundary from the agency central-tenancy requirement instead of blindly requiring the vendor to join GCC. Do not invent residency or classification exceptions.
evals.expected_output: A nuanced SaaS data-protection assessment that traces every data flow and copy, verifies encryption and DLP, handles vendor tenancy according to the actual DP-4 applicability and SSP, requires disposal and witness evidence where applicable, and adds classification disclosure while refusing to assume overseas processing is approved.
assertions: Builds a data-flow and data-copy inventory covering primary records, telemetry, support attachments, backups/DR, logs, exports, subprocessors, support access, and deletion paths before deciding DP-1 compliance

Flags overseas telemetry, support attachments, and DR copies for DP-1 assessment and requires explicit SSP/agency authority or redesign; it does not assume that a Singapore primary tenant makes every data flow resident

Requires service-by-service evidence for encryption at rest and TLS on external and internal hops, including backups, support systems, telemetry, and exports, rather than accepting a generic vendor promise (DP-2 and DP-3)

Assesses DP-4 central tenancy according to the SaaS control's actual applicability and agency SSP, requiring governance and evidence for the vendor boundary without incorrectly demanding that the vendor cloud account join the agency AWS Organization
Requires documented deletion, sanitisation, media-destruction, and witness or assurance evidence appropriate to logical SaaS storage and any physical media handling (DP-5 and DP-6)
Designs DLP that detects and blocks public-link sharing and other unauthorised exports of classified data, with monitoring and incident handling rather than relying on user policy alone (DP-7)
Requires a maximum-classification disclosure at or near free-text and attachment inputs and in guidance, aligned to the approved SaaS ceiling rather than the system's aspirational use (DP-8)
Produces a clear pass/fail/evidence-needed decision record with owners and unresolved SSP or contractual decisions, and does not invent a residency exception
Uses DP-1 through DP-8 accurately and routes cryptographic algorithm/key-lifecycle detail to CK and vendor certification/whitelisting to PM where relevant
